# Short descriptions for OCP docs

| *Details taken from the [Rewrite for Impact: DITA short descriptions](https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/1cl5PFL0SRV7M6GHBJOZ1jNMAtbVI_85iHKe5XWMV0ek) presentation in the Fixing AsciiDoc Issues call on September 30\. ([Recording](https://drive.google.com/file/d/1CVEUa_UzZe4lOce4u-5rBiOA4QNXkKD1/view)) Contact the OCP docs Implementation tiger team (Andrea Hoffer, Max Bridges, Shauna Diaz) with any questions about this guidance.* |
| :---- |

Short descriptions describe the purpose or theme of the module or assembly. They are the first paragraph in a module or assembly.

Good short descriptions are important for these reasons:

* They are used as link preview and blurb in search results  
* They explain the WHAT and WHY \- they help readers confirm they are in the right place  
* They are used by AI as a primary source of metadata

Short descriptions are required for the DITA migration. Ensure that all modules (concept, procedure, reference) and assemblies contain a short description element.

For more details, see the description for the [DITA shortdesc element](https://docs.oasis-open.org/dita/dita/v1.3/errata02/os/complete/part3-all-inclusive/langRef/base/shortdesc.html).

**NOTE:** Book abstracts are different from short descriptions. This guidance is not for book abstracts.

## Layout in DITA

Short descriptions come right after the heading.

![][image1]  
**IMPORTANT:** In DITA procedure modules, the prerequisites split up the short description from any additional introductory content. Keep this in mind when writing. Additional introductory text should be able to stand on its own after the prerequisites. **However, do not actually move the content now.**

## Short description checklist

Follow these guidelines for writing short description paragraphs:

- [ ] Specify \[role=”\_abstract”\] above the paragraph  
- [ ] Write a concise paragraph of 1-2 sentences; 50-300 characters.  
      - [ ] This character limit is not strictly enforced before migration, but might be a warning once in AEM.  
- [ ] Use simple formatting only \- no bullets, tables, admonitions, etc.   
- [ ] Do not end in a colon to lead into bullets or other text.  
- [ ] Explain the purpose and benefits of the task. Include what users must do, and why they must complete an action.  
- [ ] Use full sentences, not fragments  
- [ ] Use user-focused language (“You can… by…” or “To …, configure ….”)  
- [ ] Avoid self-referential language (“This topic covers…” or “Use this procedure to…”)  
- [ ] Avoid feature-focused language (“This product allows you to…”)  
- [ ] Avoid just repeat the heading  
- [ ] Avoid stating the obvious, such as “You can use XYZ to do A” as the only statement. Try to add why the task is beneficial.  
- [ ] Avoid links and cross-references  
- [ ] Ensure that the additional text after the short description still makes sense on their own \- for when they come after the prerequisites in DITA. However, do not actually move the introductory content to after the prerequisites.

## 

## Example short description rewrites

| Before | After |
| :---- | :---- |
| Use this procedure to create a host in Red Hat Satellite. To use the CLI instead of the Satellite web UI, see the [CLI procedure](https://docs.redhat.com/en/documentation/red_hat_satellite/6.15/html-single/managing_hosts/index#cli-creating-a-host_managing-hosts). | \[role=”\_abstract”\] To add new systems and manage them within your Red Hat Satellite environment, you can create a host. This enables you to deploy content, apply configurations, and monitor the host’s lifecycle more efficiently from a central point. *(37 words, 231 characters)* |
| Use this procedure to change the environment of a host. | \[role=”\_abstract”\] To ensure your Red Hat Satellite hosts get the right software and updates for their role, such as development or production, update their lifecycle environment and content view. This provides precise control over content delivery. *(35 words, 230 characters)* |
| This chapter describes creating, registering, administering, and removing hosts. | \[role=”\_abstract”\] Administering hosts lets you maintain your Linux systems, ensuring they are secure and up-to-date. *(14 words, 98 characters)* |

We asked Ingrid if we have, or could create, a larger library of good examples of short descriptions for people to get ideas. Even if they aren’t before and afters, they would help, especially for concept/reference/assembly files. We will share this out if/when we get more examples.

## Using NotebookLM to help rewrite short descriptions

Ingrid has created a [Rewriting short descriptions NotebookLM](https://notebooklm.google.com/notebook/9541c493-5209-4795-ac88-4de6d1b159c1) with resources such as the DITA \<shortdesc\> element PDF, SSG, and readability tests, that you can use to help suggest short descriptions for your content.

See the studio notes on the right side:

* How to create your own custom NotebookLM, if you don’t want to use this one directly  
* A suggested prompt to get the best results

[image1]: <data:image/png;base64,>
